why_correct: When a downstream system has a hard contract on output shape, format enforcement belongs at the generation boundary — the system prompt — not anywhere downstream. Explicitly declaring the JSON schema with field names and value types eliminates the ambiguity that causes Claude to fall back on prose when context feels conversational or ticket content is unusual. The CCA principle is that Claude responds to what is explicitly specified; relying on inference introduces a nondeterministic failure surface that compounds across token-level sampling decisions.

distractors.A: Few-shot examples are powerful for teaching structure, but they work by demonstrating a pattern Claude then infers — that inference step is exactly the failure mode in this scenario. A novel ticket type or an unusual phrasing can break the inferred pattern without violating any explicit instruction. Few-shot shines when you're shaping style or teaching a novel task; it's the right tool when no explicit schema can be defined, not when one already exists.
distractors.C: This is the 'vague instruction' anti-pattern — 'structured format' is underspecified to the point of being meaningless as a contract. In production, it breaks the moment Claude interprets 'structured' as a bulleted list or a markdown table, both of which satisfy the instruction and both of which will silently drop tickets. Ambiguous format directives shift the nondeterminism from token sampling to semantic interpretation, which is harder to debug.
distractors.D: Pushing format enforcement downstream inverts the trust boundary and makes the parser load-bearing for a problem that originates at generation. Permissive regex on mixed prose/JSON is brittle, untestable at scale, and masks the root cause — you end up with a fragile extraction layer that will fail on the next format variant Claude produces.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for machine-consumed output is: any field name, value type, or structural constraint that a downstream system will act on must be explicitly declared in the system prompt — never inferred, never delegated to examples alone. If the contract is binary (valid JSON or silent drop), your format instruction must be equally binary and unambiguous.
cross_domain_note: This pattern directly connects to agentic tool-use design: function calling and structured output modes exist precisely to harden this boundary at the API level, removing the instruction-compliance gap entirely for production routing pipelines.

why_correct: Claude is a generative model — absent explicit format constraints, it optimizes for coherent, contextually appropriate output, not for structural consistency across calls. The model can infer that a 'ticket summary' should be readable prose, and will produce exactly that reliably. The failure mode is that 'semantically correct' and 'structurally parseable' are orthogonal properties: Claude can nail the content while varying the container, and a 1-in-8 exception rate is the classic signature of format drift under implicit instructions. The correct pattern when integrating Claude into a typed downstream system is always to make the output contract explicit in the prompt — specify the schema, not just the task.

distractors.A: Non-determinism is real, but it's not the mechanism here. Temperature and top-p variation affect word choice and phrasing, not whether the model decides to return JSON vs. prose — that's a structural decision driven by prompt framing. A would be right if you were seeing correct JSON occasionally malformed; here you're seeing a format-class switch, which is a prompt design issue, not a sampling noise issue.
distractors.C: This inverts the trust boundary. The parser is a typed contract at a system boundary — relaxing it to accept unstructured text just moves the parsing problem downstream and introduces ambiguity into the data pipeline. The fix lives at the prompt layer, where you own the output contract, not in the consumer.
distractors.D: Few-shot examples are one valid mechanism for format control, but they're not required. Explicit format instructions in the system prompt — 'respond only with a JSON object containing summary and category keys' — are sufficient and often preferable for maintainability. Treating few-shot as mandatory for format control is the over-constraint anti-pattern.

mental_model: When Claude outputs feed a typed system boundary, treat format as a first-class prompt requirement — not a default you can rely on inference to fill. The CCA heuristic: if your downstream has a schema, your prompt has a schema. Implicit format expectations produce outputs that pass human review and break machine consumers.
cross_domain_note: This pattern recurs in agentic tool-use: function calling and structured outputs exist precisely to enforce the output contract at the model layer rather than relying on prompt inference — same architectural principle, different enforcement mechanism.

why_correct: Dynamic expertise injection is the correct pattern because it solves the calibration problem at the source — the model's instruction context — rather than at the template or post-processing layer. A single prompt with an explicit, per-request expertise constraint (e.g., 'The user is a senior software engineer; assume familiarity with REST, webhooks, and eventual consistency') gives Claude the grounding it needs to tune vocabulary, assumed knowledge, and explanation depth without duplicating prompt logic across N templates. This scales to arbitrary audience granularity — you're not locked into a binary novice/expert split — and keeps maintenance surface to one prompt. The architectural principle is: when the variable is a continuous or multi-valued user attribute, parameterize it into the prompt rather than branching at the orchestration layer.

distractors.A: Two templates looks clean, but it's the routing-layer branching anti-pattern — it works until your audience segmentation grows beyond binary. A third persona (mid-level PM, data analyst, compliance officer) requires a new template, a new routing rule, and a new maintenance burden. Dynamic injection handles arbitrary segmentation without touching prompt templates. A is correct if you have hard, stable, non-overlapping audience segments with genuinely divergent structural needs; that's not the case here.
distractors.C: This is the 'instruct for outcome, not mechanism' misconception. 'Explain clearly' gives Claude no signal about who the audience is, so it regresses to a median register — which is exactly the calibration failure the team already has in production. Vague quality directives don't substitute for explicit context about the receiver. The failure mode is reproducible and observable: support tickets don't change.
distractors.D: Appending a glossary shifts the cognitive burden to the user and doesn't fix the explanation's register mismatch. Experts still get condescending prose; novices still get confusing structure. A glossary is a documentation affordance, not a prompt calibration mechanism.

mental_model: When output quality varies by a user attribute (expertise, role, language), the CCA heuristic is: inject that attribute as an explicit constraint in the human or system turn, not as a routing branch to separate templates. Template proliferation is a maintenance smell; parameterization is the architectural solution. Ask: is this variation continuous or sparse? Continuous → parameterize. Truly discrete and structurally divergent → branch.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to personalization in agentic/RAG pipelines — when retrieved context or tool outputs need to be rendered differently per user, the same principle applies: inject persona/expertise into the synthesis prompt rather than building separate retrieval or rendering paths per segment.

why_correct: The explicit `{expertise_level}` injection forces register calibration to happen at the top of the context window, before Claude processes the substantive content of the prompt. Without it, Claude must infer audience from weak, ambiguous signals in the question body — which produces the middle-ground tone problem: not technical enough for experts, not accessible enough for novices. Explicit constraints in the system or early human turn act as hard priors that shape all downstream generation, whereas implicit inference is probabilistic and collapses to the mean of the training distribution. The architectural principle is: when you need deterministic behavioral targeting across a variable user population, inject the constraint explicitly rather than relying on inference.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is framing a prompt engineering decision as a token-cost tradeoff when the real issue is output consistency. A is tempting because token budget is a legitimate CCA concern, but the stem is about correctness of calibration, not cost optimization. In production onboarding, a novice receiving expert-register output is a UX failure, not a cost inefficiency — the framing is wrong, not just the priority.
distractors.C: This is the inference-is-sufficient misconception. It's true Claude can partially infer expertise from phrasing, but 'I want to understand how neural networks work' gives almost no signal — it's asked by PhD students and curious teenagers alike. Relying on inference introduces non-determinism into a template designed for consistent output, and it completely breaks when the question itself is ambiguous or domain-neutral. Calling the placeholder 'documentation only' misunderstands the mechanism: the token is a functional prior, not a comment.
distractors.D: There is no Anthropic content policy requiring audience identification in explanatory prompts. This conflates usage policy constraints with prompt engineering best practices — a category error.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: explicit system-level constraints produce deterministic behavioral targeting; implicit inference produces probabilistic regression to the mean. Any time you need consistent register, tone, or persona across a variable input population, inject the constraint rather than hoping the model infers it. If you can't point to the token that enforces the behavior, you don't control the behavior.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary domain: explicit constraints in the system prompt sit above the trust boundary and cannot be overridden by user-turn content, whereas inferred behavior from the human turn is malleable — relevant when a sophisticated user's phrasing could unintentionally (or intentionally) shift the register away from what the product requires.

why_correct: Positive instructions define the intended output space but don't close off adjacent failure modes that Claude has generalized tendencies toward — speculative language is a deeply trained behavior that surfaces in summarization tasks because it reads as helpful elaboration. The architectural principle is that positive and negative constraints operate on different dimensions: positive instructions shape what to produce, negative constraints explicitly suppress competing behaviors the model has learned to default to. In high-stakes agentic contexts where attorneys act on output before review, you need both layers because the cost of a false negative (speculation slipping through) is asymmetric. This is the dual-constraint pattern — necessary when the failure mode is a trained tendency, not just an ambiguity in the task definition.

distractors.A: Near-miss. Tightening the positive instruction does reduce ambiguity and would help, but it still doesn't explicitly suppress the failure mode. Claude can satisfy 'extract and restate only verbatim facts' while still appending speculative inference as supplementary context — it doesn't read those as mutually exclusive. A would be the right call if the issue were task ambiguity; the issue here is a competing generalized tendency that requires explicit suppression.
distractors.C: The misconception is that instruction prominence — position, capitalization, formatting — is a primary lever for compliance. Formatting draws attention but doesn't increase the binding force of an instruction or suppress competing behaviors. In production, this breaks when the failure mode is a trained tendency rather than an attention or salience problem. The architectural tell is that attorneys are seeing consistent speculative language, not random drift — that pattern indicates a behavioral tendency, not a parsing issue.
distractors.D: Replacing positive guidance entirely with prohibitions removes the behavioral target. Without a clear affirmative instruction, you're relying on Claude to infer the desired output from a list of exclusions — which increases ambiguity and can produce outputs that are technically compliant but useless. Negative constraints are suppressors, not constructors.

mental_model: When a failure mode is consistent and pattern-matched (not random), it signals a trained tendency, not a prompt ambiguity — and trained tendencies require explicit negative constraints to suppress, not just a more precise positive instruction. The CCA heuristic: positive instructions alone are sufficient when the task is underspecified; dual constraints are required when the model has a competing generalized behavior that overlaps with the task.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to trust boundary design in agentic systems — when Claude is a node in a pipeline where downstream actors take real-world action on its output, the suppression of hallucinated or speculative content becomes a safety constraint, not just a quality preference. That context should trigger constitutional-style guardrails at the prompt layer, not just output filtering.

why_correct: Positive instructions define the target behavior but leave the model's default generalization behaviors active — in this case, the tendency to fill narrative gaps with inferred intent. Negative constraints work by explicitly closing off a behavioral path that the model would otherwise traverse on edge cases where the positive instruction is ambiguous. The v2 result demonstrates that 'summarize accurately' and 'do not speculate' occupy different parts of the instruction space: one sets a goal, the other constrains the path to that goal. This is the positive/negative constraint complementarity pattern — both are load-bearing in production prompts that must survive edge-case inputs.

distractors.A: The architectural tell against A is 'always' and 'never' — this is a universal claim from a two-input experiment. A is tempting because the lab result genuinely supports adding negative constraints here, but the correct inference is domain- and failure-mode-specific. In production, many domains never surface the relevant failure modes and negative constraints would be noise that competes with positive signal.
distractors.C: This inverts the causality. The rubric flagged speculation, v2 eliminated speculation — that's the rubric working correctly as a behavioral contract validator. The misconception is treating rubric sensitivity as a measurement artifact rather than as evidence that the prompt changed the model's output distribution. In production, dismissing rubric signal this way leads to prompt debt: you stop iterating on the prompt and start blaming the eval.
distractors.D: Prompt length is not a meaningful independent variable here. The failure mode is attributing improvement to a correlated surface feature — token count — rather than to the semantic function of the added constraint. In production, this mental model produces bloated prompts that add length without closing specific failure modes.

mental_model: When an eval flags a specific failure mode on edge cases that your positive instruction doesn't catch, the fix is almost always a targeted negative constraint — not a rewrite of the positive instruction. The CCA heuristic: positive instructions set the goal, negative constraints close the escape routes that edge-case inputs exploit.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to system prompt architecture in agentic loops — positive instructions define tool-use goals, but negative constraints (e.g., 'do not call external APIs without user confirmation') are what actually enforce trust boundaries when the orchestration layer hits ambiguous states.

why_correct: The rule 12/31 conflict is a symptom of prompt brittleness caused by flat-enumeration architecture — adding rule 48 treats the symptom, not the structural deficiency. A principled hierarchy gives Claude a reasoning substrate for novel conflict resolution: when two lower-level rules collide, the model can invoke the higher-priority principle to adjudicate rather than pattern-matching against an ever-growing lookup table. This is the correct pattern when rule density grows to the point where inter-rule interactions are no longer auditable by humans, because the model's generalization capability becomes an asset rather than a liability. The architectural tell is '18 months of incident-driven accretion' — that trajectory never terminates cleanly without structural intervention.

distractors.A: Adding rule 48 with a formal triage process institutionalizes the anti-pattern — it trades one conflict pair for an n*(n-1)/2 conflict surface that scales quadratically with every future rule. This approach is correct when the rule space is genuinely closed and enumerable; fintech transaction categorization is neither, especially under evolving regulatory regimes.
distractors.C: Few-shot grounding shifts the conflict from explicit rules to implicit distributional learning, but doesn't eliminate it — it just makes conflicts invisible and non-auditable, which is catastrophically worse in a regulatory context. Compliance auditors need traceable decision logic; implicit few-shot judgment fails that requirement the moment a novel transaction type falls outside the example distribution.
distractors.D: Parallel single-rule classifiers eliminate intra-prompt conflict by destroying the model's ability to reason about rule interactions at all — vote aggregation has no mechanism to encode that rule 31 should override rule 12 in specific contexts, so the miscategorization becomes deterministic rather than occasional.

mental_model: When a prompt's rule count grows through incident-driven accretion and inter-rule conflicts start surfacing, the correct refactor is always hierarchical principle extraction — not conflict-specific patching. The CCA heuristic: if you can't fit the rule interaction graph in your head, neither can your prompt, and adding rules makes this worse not better.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic system design: tool-call schemas and agent constraints face the same flat-enumeration failure mode, where a hierarchy of guardrail priorities outperforms an exhaustive prohibition list as the action space grows.

why_correct: When a constraint set scales beyond ~10 rules, implicit priority resolution breaks down because Claude has no principled basis for adjudicating same-transaction conflicts — it falls back on positional heuristics or recency bias within the context window. The correct pattern is to encode an explicit priority lattice directly in the prompt architecture, so conflict resolution is deterministic rather than emergent. This is the constraint hierarchy pattern: give Claude the meta-rule that governs rule-rule conflicts, and novel edge cases resolve correctly without requiring exhaustive exemplar coverage.

distractors.A: Temperature controls stochastic sampling, not rule priority resolution. Raising it would make output less stable across identical inputs, not more coherent — you'd get random rule selection rather than principled selection. The failure mode here is nondeterminism masquerading as exploration; in production, this makes your score_response() variance worse, not better.
distractors.C: This is the near-miss. Flagging conflicts instead of resolving them shifts the arbitration burden to your orchestration layer, which sounds principled but produces a dead end in a live categorization pipeline — you still need a resolution strategy, and now you've added latency and downstream handling complexity. It addresses the symptom (conflict detection) while ignoring the root cause (no priority order). The correct pattern resolves conflicts at inference time, not post-hoc.
distractors.D: Few-shot patching is an implicit encoding strategy — you're teaching resolution behavior through examples rather than stating the rule. This works for isolated cases but doesn't generalize: each newly discovered conflict requires a new example, and at 47 constraints the combinatorial conflict space grows faster than your example block can cover. In production, this is the whack-a-mole anti-pattern; it looks right until a novel conflict pair appears that has no exemplar.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for constraint-scale failures is: when score degradation correlates with rule-rule conflict, the fix is always structural (add a priority lattice), never statistical (temperature) or additive (more examples). If Claude is making an ordering decision, give it an ordering rule — don't hope sampling or exemplars converge on the right one.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic tool-use design: when an agent has multiple tools with overlapping applicability, you need an explicit tool-selection priority policy in the system prompt, not implicit resolution — the same constraint hierarchy principle applies at the orchestration layer.
